problem_summary: 2024 MCM Problem B (discrete): design a 4-step safety/rescue procedure (Locate, Prepare, Search, Extrapolate) for a tourist submersible that loses contact with its host ship in the Ionian Sea. Must (1) predict the lost submersible's position over time and quantify uncertainty, naming onboard data/equipment that reduces it; (2) recommend search equipment for host ship and rescue vessels under cost/availability/maintenance/readiness/usage trade-offs; (3) build a search-deployment model that integrates the location prediction to minimize time-to-find and compute the cumulative probability of finding the submersible vs. time; (4) extrapolate to other regions (Caribbean) and to multiple simultaneously-lost submersibles. Team 2407038's solution splits this into three coupled models (trajectory dynamics, multi-objective equipment allocation, grid/Bayesian search) and closes with a memo to the Greek government.
models_used: Model I - Trajectory Prediction via Dynamics Analysis: real CMEMS/NOAA/GEBCO ocean-current, density and seafloor data feeding a Newtonian force-balance ODE (gravity F=mg, buoyancy F=rho*g*V, drag F=0.5*rho*v^2*Cd*A, plus current advection v=vs+vc), integrated numerically in Python with a seafloor lower-bound constraint
ARIMA(p,d,q) time-series model (Box-Jenkins) to forecast the 3 components (N-S, E-W, vertical) of current velocity over time, with differencing for stationarity and residual checks
Monte Carlo simulation to quantify forecast uncertainty: feed N(mu,sigma^2) random input series through the fitted ARIMA, take max relative residual U=max R_t per run, average to U_avg = 2.39%
Ridge regression (L2-regularized, MATLAB ridge(y,X,lambda)) to interpolate a continuous 3D field of current velocity (3 directions) and seawater density from discrete sampled grid points

Model II - Multi-objective optimization (CEAOM) solved by a Genetic Algorithm (NSGA-style) producing a Pareto front over 3 objectives: minimize cost f_C = sum(Ci*xi + (Mi/Ui)*xi), maximize availability f_A, minimize prep time f_R = max(Ri*xi); binary purchase decision variables xi over 5 equipment types
Availability sub-model: Environmental Coefficient EC = Range/Sensitivity, multiplied across Water Temp, Depth, Current, Noise, Interference coefficients (WTC*WDC*WCC*NC*IC)
Model III - Grid Probability Search: rasterize search surface into ~300m x 300m cells (grid size derived from USNS Grasp 25-knot speed, 5m swath, 20% overlap, 30-min sweep)
Poisson distribution P(X=k)=e^-lambda*lambda^k/k! over Manhattan distance from the predicted central grid, with time-decaying intensity lambda = m + ln(t/t0) to model spreading uncertainty

Bayesian posterior update: prior = Poisson probability; after sweeping a cell set its probability to 0 (target movement < grid size justifies no re-search), renormalize via p(A|B)=Poisson/p(B); accumulate find-probability over 30-min intervals across n vessels
Spherical-distance coordinate conversion (lat/lon arc to meters) to put data into SI units for the mechanics model
Extrapolation: re-run Model I on Caribbean data; multi-submersible handling by summing per-cell probabilities (expected target count) across independent submersibles
key_techniques: Physics-first 'computational dynamics simulation' over a black-box LSTM - explicitly justified in the literature review as more transparent and lower-cost, fitting a discrete/explainable problem
Layering data-driven components (ARIMA forecast + ridge-regression field interpolation) as inputs INTO a mechanistic ODE rather than replacing it - hybrid statistical-physical pipeline
Sourcing real external datasets (Copernicus CMEMS currents, NOAA NCEI density, GEBCO bathymetry) instead of fabricating numbers - grounds the model in a problem with no provided data
Monte Carlo wrapper around the forecaster to turn 'identify uncertainties' into a single defensible number (2.39%)
Using Poisson over Manhattan distance as a clean discrete spatial probability prior centered on the predicted location - directly ties Model I output into Model III

Bayesian zero-out-and-renormalize search scheme with a physical justification (current speed 0.12 m/s -> drift 216m < 300m grid in one interval -> no benefit to re-searching) producing a monotonically rising cumulative-find-probability curve

Genetic-algorithm Pareto front then post-filtered to integer schemes, then mapped to a '1+n' fleet doctrine (host ship = full kit/command, fast boats = minimal kit/low readiness, intermediate = balanced)
Closing the loop on all four problem verbs with a named sub-model each, and ending with the required memo to the Greek government
why_it_won: "It is end-to-end coherent and complete: every one of the four required verbs (Locate/Prepare/Search/Extrapolate) maps to a named, self-contained model, and the models are genuinely coupled - the predicted trajectory becomes the Poisson central grid, whose uncertainty feeds the Bayesian search. Judges reward solutions where Part 3 actually consumes Part 1's output rather than three disconnected essays. The team did the unglamorous work of collecting real oceanographic data (Copernicus, NOAA, GEBCO) for a problem that ships no data, which signals seriousness. It chose a transparent physics-based dynamics model over a fashionable neural net AND defended that choice in the literature review, showing modeling judgment rather than tool-chasing. Uncertainty is quantified concretely (2.39% via Monte Carlo), the search produces an interpretable, decision-relevant artifact (cumulative find-probability vs. time, showing the 'golden hour' diminishing returns from 0.55 at 5h to 0.72 at 50h), and the actionable '1+n' fleet recommendation reads like real operational advice. Scalability (Caribbean east-west current match) and sensitivity (final probability changes <5.8% for 10% time-interval change) are both demonstrated, and the deliverable memo satisfies the prompt's communication requirement."
section_highlights: "Strong scaffolding: a workflow flowchart (Fig 1), an explicit Assumptions section with per-assumption justifications, a Notations table, and a dedicated Model Preparation section (data sources + lat/lon-to-meter conversion) before any modeling. The literature review is unusually purposeful - it frames the two real choices (neural-net vs. dynamics for prediction; random/geometric/heuristic for search) and uses them to motivate the team's picks, citing prior MCM-relevant work (EM static-target, RRT multi-target). Each model follows a clean problem->equation->result->figure rhythm with numbered equations (35 of them) and result tables. The cumulative-probability curve and the sensitivity bar chart are the standout decision-relevant visuals. The paper ends with the required government memo restating each procedure in plain operational language with a host-ship/rescue-boat equipment matrix - directly answering the prompt's deliverable. Consistent four-verb spine (Locate/Prepare/Search/Extrapolate) threads the entire paper from restatement to memo."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The ARIMA/Monte-Carlo 'uncertainty' figure (2.39%) is suspect: the uncertainty plot (Fig 4) shows ~0 everywhere, and feeding random N(mu,sigma^2) noise as ARIMA inputs is not a rigorous propagation of current-field uncertainty into final position error - it under-reports true trajectory uncertainty

The dynamics ODE system (Eq 18) has an apparent typo: dvx/dt and dvy/dt are written with the identical RHS (-FR*cos(phi)*cos(theta)), so the x and y drag decomposition is inconsistent - the theta azimuth term is duplicated rather than split into cos/sin

Assumptions 3-5 are very strong and convenient: zero propulsion after failure, fixed mass/volume, and a rescue vessel that detects the target with probability 1 whenever co-located (Assumption 5 sets miss probability to 0) - this idealizes away the hardest part of real underwater search (detection/sensor performance)

lambda = m + ln(t/t0) for the Poisson intensity is an ad-hoc, unvalidated choice; the Poisson-over-Manhattan-distance prior is asserted rather than derived from the trajectory's actual probability density, so Model III is only loosely connected to Model I's real physics

Availability via EC = Range/Sensitivity and a bare product of five coefficients is hand-wavy and dimensionally opaque; the equipment data table mixes wildly different magnitudes (availability ~1e6) without normalization shown

Genetic algorithm details are thin - no population size, generations, crossover/mutation operators, or convergence evidence; the 'Pareto front' 3D scatter is sparse and the cost-vs-readiness relationship looks nearly linear/synthetic

Several embedded figures appear to be placeholder/garbled (the giant Fig-1 mermaid has dozens of meaningless 'Coordinate/Matrix' and 'Prediction' nodes; trajectory line-chart tables have only 2-7 points), suggesting figures were illustrative rather than reproducibly generated
Multi-submersible extension is asserted ('only small changes needed', sum the probabilities) but fairness across submersibles is acknowledged as unsolved and punted to 'more complex algorithms'
reusable_patterns: Four-verb prompt -> four named sub-models spine: mirror the problem's own action words through restatement, each modeling section, and the final memo so completeness is visually obvious to judges

Hybrid 'data feeds physics' pipeline: use statistical forecasters (ARIMA) + field interpolation (ridge regression) to supply parameters to a mechanistic ODE, instead of choosing pure-ML vs pure-physics
Make 'identify uncertainty' concrete with a Monte Carlo wrapper that outputs one headline number, then sanity-check that the number is actually meaningful (the cautionary half of this lesson)
Connect a trajectory/prediction model to a search/decision model by converting the predicted point into a spatial probability prior (Poisson over Manhattan distance centered on the prediction)

Bayesian search-with-elimination: zero out swept cells and renormalize, JUSTIFIED by a physical drift calculation (target speed x interval < cell size), yielding a clean monotone cumulative-find-probability deliverable
For 'no data provided' problems, collect and cite real domain datasets (Copernicus/NOAA/GEBCO) and show a coordinate/unit-conversion step - signals rigor
Multi-objective resource allocation: GA -> Pareto front -> integer post-filter -> map solutions to a human-readable doctrine ('1+n' fleet with role-tiered kits)

Always demonstrate BOTH scalability (re-run on a second region and cross-check against a known real-world fact, e.g. Caribbean east-west current) and sensitivity (perturb one key parameter +/-10%, report bounded output change), then deliver the required communication artifact (memo) restating results operationally
